items.question: Define a matrix and explain when two matrices can be added.
items.answer: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers with m rows and n columns. Two matrices can be added if and only if they have exactly the same dimensions m x n, in which case the sum is formed by adding corresponding entries.

items.question: What is the transpose of a matrix, and how does it affect the matrix shape?
items.answer: The transpose of a matrix A, denoted A^T, is obtained by swapping its rows and columns so that the entry in row i, column j of A becomes the entry in row j, column i of A^T. If A is m x n, then A^T is n x m.

items.question: Why do we require matching dimensions for matrix addition but not for scalar multiplication?
items.answer: Matrix addition is defined entrywise, so every entry in one matrix must have a corresponding entry in the other, which requires identical dimensions. Scalar multiplication multiplies each entry by the same scalar, so it applies to any single matrix regardless of its dimensions.

items.question: Give examples of at least two special kinds of matrices and explain why they are useful.
items.answer: An identity matrix has 1 on the diagonal and 0 elsewhere and acts as a multiplicative identity for matrices of the same size, making it essential for defining inverses and neutral transformations. A diagonal matrix has non-zero entries only on the main diagonal, which makes many operations, such as applying it to a vector, simple and fast because each component is scaled independently.
